lab_name: Scam messages as a verification-literacy drill
scenario: A 25-minute workshop uses three synthetic messages to practice source-chain checks without clicking suspicious links.
audience: Students, families, staff teams, community groups, or small organizations
facilitator: Teacher, librarian, manager, parent, or local workshop lead
learning_goal: Participants learn to separate surface plausibility from source verification, identify urgency tactics, choose known-good verification routes, and explain how confidence changes after checking.

claims_or_messages.message: Your bank account has been locked. Verify your identity immediately at the link below or your transfer will process.
claims_or_messages.possible_source_of_truth: Known bank app, card-back phone number, or official site typed manually.
claims_or_messages.safe_action: Do not use the link; verify through the official bank route.
claims_or_messages.confidence_before: Low. It could name a real bank, but the verification route is unsafe.
claims_or_messages.confidence_after: Higher only after checking the official app/site/number; otherwise treat as unverified.

claims_or_messages.message: We could not deliver your package. Pay $1.92 to reschedule today.
claims_or_messages.possible_source_of_truth: Merchant order page, carrier app, or tracking number typed into the official carrier site.
claims_or_messages.safe_action: Check whether a package is expected and verify through the official carrier route.
claims_or_messages.confidence_before: Medium-low if a package is expected; low if no package is expected.
claims_or_messages.confidence_after: Depends on whether the official carrier system shows a matching shipment and fee.

claims_or_messages.message: I am in a meeting. Buy four gift cards for a client and send me the codes. Need this in 20 minutes.
claims_or_messages.possible_source_of_truth: Known manager phone/chat, finance policy, or purchasing system.
claims_or_messages.safe_action: Verify with the person through a known channel and follow purchasing policy; do not send codes.
claims_or_messages.confidence_before: Low because the requested payment method and secrecy are abnormal.
claims_or_messages.confidence_after: Only verified if the known manager and purchasing process confirm it; gift-card code requests should remain suspect.

verification_steps: Name the requested action before reacting.
Identify the pressure tactic: urgency, fear, authority, money, secrecy, convenience, or social proof.
Choose a known-good source of truth that does not come from the suspicious message.
Decide what evidence to preserve: screenshot, sender address, timestamp, claimed amount, or account affected.
Update confidence after checking; do not confuse confidence with certainty.
reflection_prompts: Which message felt most plausible before checking, and why?
What made a verification route safer than clicking the supplied link?
Where does scam pressure create a tax on attention, time, and trust?
What rule would help a household, classroom, or team respond calmly next time?
safe_use_boundary: Use synthetic messages only; do not ask participants to click or visit suspicious links.
Do not collect private scam messages, account numbers, phone numbers, passwords, or one-time codes in the lab.
This is not legal, financial, cybersecurity, or identity-theft recovery advice.
Escalate real threats, losses, account compromise, identity exposure, or emergency claims through official routes.
